topic: Async - JoinSet for Dynamic Task Sets

turns.content: I'm looking at the new codebase and I'm confused about why we're using tokio::task::JoinSet instead of just spawning tasks individually. It feels like it should be simpler to just use a vector of handles, right? Why does this dynamic approach even make sense when my current job uses standard futures?

Great question. The core difference is that JoinSet acts like a single synchronization point for an entire collection of tasks, whereas a vector of handles gives you backends with no built-in coordination mechanism. Think of it like this: if you have a fleet of drones flying in formation, using JoinSet is like having one ground control tower that can instantly tell all the drones to stop and report their status together without needing to send individual radio messages to each drone. With a vector of handles, you'd need to manage every single connection manually, which gets messy quickly when tasks are added or removed dynamically.
Okay so I can add tasks on the fly with this? Like if a task fails, does it automatically remove the rest from the set?

Exactly. That's one of its main strengths - you can abort remaining tasks when an error occurs by calling the interrupt method on the JoinSet itself. It's like pulling a plug on the entire formation; all other drones stop immediately without needing individual commands. This is perfect for scenarios where you need to cancel pending work based on a single failure condition.
Wait, so if I have ten tasks running and one fails, can I just call abort() and they all stop?

Yes, that's exactly how it works. You call the interrupt method on the JoinSet object after detecting an error in one task. It signals cancellation to every pending task in the set simultaneously without needing individual handles or complex state management.
I see, so this is basically replacing my manual wait loops? Does it handle waiting for completions automatically?

Yes, the JoinSet handles both adding tasks and waiting for their completion in one unified interface. You can add new tasks to the set at runtime as needed, then simply wait on the entire collection until all tasks complete or are aborted.
